title: Seasons Through My Spout
synopsis: A watering can narrates the hilarious year-long adventures of Hoot, a tiny round baby owl, as he tries to fix his beloved weather station in a secret rooftop garden. Through spring's sprouting chaos, summer's melting mishaps, autumn's flying fiasco, and winter's freezing finale, Hoot learns that sometimes the best solutions come from working together with friends.

pages.text:
My copper spout has been dripping for exactly three hundred and twelve days, and today something extraordinary happened. The little weather station that sits beside my handle started making the most dreadful grinding noise—CLUNK-wheeze-BONK—like a hiccupping robot with rusty joints. Hoot, the tiny round baby owl with speckled cream-and-brown feathers, enormous golden eyes, and tufty ear feathers, fluttered down from his perch on the rain gauge, his golden eyes wide with worry.

"Oh no, oh no!" Hoot hooted, his tufty ear feathers quivering. "My weather station is broken! How will I measure the spring rain and wind?"

The rooftop garden around us buzzed with the sounds of the city below—honking horns, chattering people, rumbling buses. But up here among the tomato plants and sunflower seedlings, we had our own scientific world.

illustration.description: A tiny round baby owl with speckled cream-and-brown feathers, enormous golden eyes, and tufty ear feathers examines a smoking, broken weather station in a lush rooftop garden. Scientific instruments are scattered around blooming spring flowers while a concerned watering can watches. The scene shows scientific wonder with bubbling reactions and wide-eyed discovery.
illustration.alt: Hoot examining his broken weather station in the spring garden

pages.text:
Hoot tried everything. He tapped the weather vane with his tiny talons—BOING! It spun backward. He blew on the thermometer—WHOOSH! The numbers scrambled like dancing ants. He even tried sitting on the barometer, but that just made it beep the wrong temperatures in three different languages.

"Maybe I need helpers," Hoot whispered to himself, but being a nocturnal little owl, he usually worked alone. The spring breeze carried the scent of fresh basil and growing beans, and I felt my handle warm in the morning sun. This was going to be quite an adventure.

From my spot by the herb garden, I watched Hoot's golden eyes grow even larger as he stared at his precious weather station. Spring was the perfect time for new beginnings—but some beginnings required more than one pair of wings.

illustration.description: A tiny round baby owl with speckled cream-and-brown feathers, enormous golden eyes, and tufty ear feathers attempts various fixes on weather instruments, with sparks and steam creating colorful experiments around him. The rooftop garden blooms with spring vegetables while scientific equipment shows eureka moments gone wrong.
illustration.alt: Hoot trying unsuccessfully to fix his weather equipment

pages.text:
By summer, things had gotten spectacularly worse. The July heat made my metal sides too hot to touch, and Hoot's broken weather station now looked like a science experiment gone magnificently wrong. Wires sprouted like spaghetti, dials pointed in impossible directions, and the wind gauge spun so fast it hummed opera.

Hoot recruited Chirp the cricket and Buzz the bee for what he called "Operation Fix-It." I watched through the steam rising from my sun-warmed water as the three friends gathered around the weather station with tiny tools—a paperclip, a bottle cap, and one very determined dandelion stem.

"According to my calculations," Hoot announced importantly, adjusting an imaginary pair of glasses with his wing, "we need to recalibrate the atmospheric pressure sensor." None of them knew what that meant, but it sounded very scientific.

illustration.description: A tiny round baby owl with speckled cream-and-brown feathers, enormous golden eyes, and tufty ear feathers works alongside a cricket and a bee around a comically over-complicated weather station. Steam rises from scientific equipment in the summer heat of the rooftop garden, with tomatoes and peppers growing nearby. The scene shows microscope views and amazing revelations.
illustration.alt: Hoot and friends attempting repairs in the hot summer garden

pages.text:
What happened next was pure summer chaos. Chirp accidentally cricket-jumped onto the reset button seventeen times. Buzz got dizzy from flying around the spinning wind gauge and crashed into the rain collector—SPLASH! And Hoot, trying to be helpful, stuck his entire head inside the main control box and got completely stuck.

"HOOT! HOOT! HOOT!" came his muffled voice from inside the machine, while his tufty ear feathers wiggled frantically outside. His golden eyes, when they finally emerged, were crossed from looking at circuits up close.

The weather station was now making sounds like a kazoo orchestra, and I couldn't help but giggle—if watering cans can giggle—at the wonderful disaster unfolding among the summer squash and buzzing bees.

illustration.description: A tiny round baby owl with speckled cream-and-brown feathers, enormous golden eyes, and tufty ear feathers has his head stuck in a weather station while a cricket and bee create chaos around bubbling scientific equipment. The summer rooftop garden shows colorful experiments gone wrong with science as magic.
illustration.alt: Comedy chaos as Hoot gets stuck while his friends cause more problems

pages.text:
Autumn arrived with a whoosh that nearly knocked me over, and the rooftop garden transformed into a carpet of orange and red leaves. Hoot's weather station had developed new problems—it now played polka music every time the wind blew, and the temperature gauge only worked upside-down.

"I have a brilliant idea!" Hoot announced one crisp October morning, his breath making little puffs in the cool air. "If the problem is inside the machine, maybe I need to approach it from above!" He convinced Swoosh the autumn leaf and Whirl the mini-tornado (don't ask me how he befriended a tornado) to help him fly directly into the weather station's sensors.

What could possibly go wrong?

illustration.description: A tiny round baby owl with speckled cream-and-brown feathers, enormous golden eyes, and tufty ear feathers prepares for an aerial approach to his weather station among swirling autumn leaves and mini-whirlwinds. The rooftop garden shows fall vegetables and scientific equipment with wide-eyed discovery moments.
illustration.alt: Hoot planning an aerial attack on his weather problems in autumn

pages.text:
Everything went wrong in the most magnificent way possible. Hoot flew straight into the weather vane—CLANG! Swoosh the leaf got sucked into the wind gauge—WHOMP! And Whirl the mini-tornado spun everything so fast that weather data from next Tuesday somehow appeared on the screen.

Hoot tumbled through the air like a feathery pinwheel, his tufty ear feathers streaming behind him like tiny flags. He landed headfirst in my bowl with a tremendous SPLASH, sending water flying in all directions. When he popped up, sputtering and soaked, his usually round shape looked more like a wet cotton ball with enormous golden eyes.

"Maybe," Hoot said quietly, water dripping from his speckled cream-and-brown feathers, "I need a completely different approach."

illustration.description: A tiny round baby owl with speckled cream-and-brown feathers, enormous golden eyes, and tufty ear feathers sits soaking wet in a watering can while autumn leaves and scientific equipment swirl chaotically around the rooftop garden. The scene shows slapstick eureka moments with amazing revelations gone wrong.
illustration.alt: Soaked Hoot sitting in the watering can after his flying mishap

pages.text:
As Hoot sat shivering in my bowl, something wonderful happened. All his friends—Chirp the cricket, Buzz the bee, Swoosh the leaf, and even Whirl the mini-tornado—gathered around us. They didn't try to fix anything. They didn't make suggestions. They just sat with the soggy little owl, keeping him company as the first winter snowflakes began to fall.

"You know what?" Hoot said softly, his golden eyes reflecting the early stars. "Maybe the weather station doesn't need to work perfectly. Maybe it just needs to work with friends." And as if it heard him, the machine gave one gentle, musical chime—the most beautiful sound it had made all year.

illustration.description: A tiny round baby owl with speckled cream-and-brown feathers, enormous golden eyes, and tufty ear feathers sits surrounded by his friends in the snowy rooftop garden as the weather station glows softly. The scene shows scientific wonder with gentle eureka moments and the magic of teamwork.
illustration.alt: Hoot and all his friends gathering around the peacefully glowing weather station

coverPrompt.description: A tiny round baby owl with speckled cream-and-brown feathers, enormous golden eyes, and tufty ear feathers stands atop a colorful rooftop garden filled with scientific instruments and blooming plants. The city skyline stretches behind while a smiling watering can sits nearby. Scientific wonder fills the scene with bubbling experiments and amazing revelations.
coverPrompt.alt: Cover showing Hoot the baby owl in a rooftop garden with scientific equipment

questions.question: What tells the story of Hoot's adventures?
questions.explanation: The watering can narrates the entire story, watching Hoot's adventures through all four seasons.

questions.question: What was broken that Hoot wanted to fix?
questions.explanation: Hoot's beloved weather station was making strange noises and giving wrong readings.

questions.question: In which season did Hoot get his head stuck in the machine?
questions.explanation: During the hot summer, Hoot got his head completely stuck in the weather station's control box.

questions.question: Where did Hoot land after his flying mishap in autumn?
questions.explanation: Hoot tumbled through the air and landed headfirst in the watering can with a big splash.

questions.question: How did the weather station finally work better?
questions.explanation: When all of Hoot's friends gathered around, the weather station gave a beautiful, gentle chime—showing that things work better with friends.
